notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Your May spending data shows some clear patterns worth optimizing around. Let me work through your actual spending across those five categories and match them to your two cards — the American Express Gold Card (1005) and Chase Freedom Unlimited (2094).
Your May spending in those categories
From your transactions, here's what you spent in May across the categories you mentioned:
Groceries: $1,023.54 — King Soopers ($286.74), Costco ($736.80 across two trips)
Dining: $164.75 — Blue Pan Pizza ($78.35), Snooze AM Eatery ($86.40)
Childcare/daycare: $1,870 — Bright Horizons ($1,650), Care.com backup ($220)
Utilities: $164.22 — Xcel Energy
Card-by-card breakdown
Since your profile doesn't include your specific rewards rates for each card, I'll base this on each card's well-known published reward structure, which you can verify against your cardmember agreement.
American Express Gold Card is typically outstanding for food-related spending, offering 4x points at U.S. supermarkets (up to $25K/year) and 4x at restaurants worldwide. For Costco specifically, Amex Gold generally earns only 1x — Costco operates as a warehouse club, not a supermarket, so the 4x grocery bonus typically doesn't apply there.
Chase Freedom Unlimited typically earns 1.5% cash back on all purchases, with 3% on dining and drugstores. It's straightforward and consistent across all merchants.
My recommendations by category
Groceries (King Soopers): Amex Gold — 4x points at U.S. supermarkets is a strong advantage over Chase's 1.5%
Dining (restaurants): Amex Gold — 4x at restaurants beats Chase's 3% dining rate
Costco: Chase Freedom Unlimited — Costco doesn't accept Amex cards in-store, so Chase is your only option there. (Costco.com does accept Amex, but warehouse locations are Visa-only.)
Daycare (Bright Horizons, Care.com): Chase Freedom Unlimited — childcare providers typically don't qualify for Amex's bonus categories, so Chase's flat 1.5% applies either way. Chase at least keeps it simple.
Utilities (Xcel Energy): Chase Freedom Unlimited — utilities don't earn bonus rates on Amex Gold either, so Chase's 1.5% flat rate is equivalent or slightly better depending on how you value Amex points.
The big picture
Your groceries and dining together totaled about $1,188 in May — that's where Amex Gold earns its keep most powerfully. Costco alone was $737, and since Amex isn't accepted there, Chase is the default. For daycare at $1,870/month, neither card offers a bonus category, so the decision there is more about which points/cash back currency you prefer to accumulate.
Would you like to dig into whether the Amex Gold's annual fee still makes sense given your actual spending mix, or explore whether a different card might better cover daycare and utilities?
